questions.question: Differentiate between started services and bound services in Android with examples.
keyPoints: Started service launched via startService(), runs independently
Bound service launched via bindService(), provides client-server interface
Started service stops via stopSelf() or stopService()
Bound service destroyed when all clients unbind

questions.question: Explain the complete Android Service lifecycle with a diagram, covering both started and bound service paths.
keyPoints: onCreate() called when service is first created
Started path: onStartCommand() called for each startService() invocation
Bound path: onBind() returns IBinder, onUnbind() when clients disconnect
onDestroy() called when service is no longer needed
Lifecycle diagram showing both started and bound paths
Discussion of onStartCommand() return values (START_STICKY, START_NOT_STICKY, START_REDELIVER_INTENT)

questions.question: What is IntentService? Explain its advantages and how it differs from a regular Service.
keyPoints: IntentService automatically creates a worker thread
Processes intents sequentially in a queue
Auto-stops when all requests are handled
Regular Service runs on the main thread and requires manual thread management

questions.question: Explain foreground services in Android. Why are they needed and how do you implement one?
keyPoints: Foreground services perform user-noticeable operations
Must display a persistent notification
Higher priority than background services, less likely to be killed
Implementation using startForeground() with a Notification object
Must declare foregroundServiceType in manifest for Android 10+
Use cases: music playback, location tracking, file downloads
